title-group.article-title: Understanding adolescent self-harm attempts in Ghana: a qualitative analysis of family and social risk factors

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/054tfvs49grid.449729.50000 0004 7707 5975Fred N. Binka School of Public Health, University of Health and Allied Sciences, Ho, Ghana
3Christain Health Association of Ghana, Techiman, Ghana

5https://ror.org/04tvaz8810000 0005 0598 6785Department of Environment and Public Health, School of Natural and Environmental Sciences, University of Environment and Sustainable Development, Somanya, Ghana

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Adolescent suicide is a serious global public health concern. In nations with limited resources like Ghana, an understanding of the risk factors for attempted suicide is crucial to the effectiveness of public health treatments. Relevant Ghanaian research has documented the prevalence of suicidal behaviour, particularly among in-school adolescents. However, the causes of adolescents’ suicide attempts have not received enough in-depth investigation. In two Ghanaian municipalities, this study examined the family and social factors linked to suicide attempts in adolescents between the ages of 15 and 19 years.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Twenty (20) adolescents with a history of attempted suicide were identified, information about their experiences was gathered through in-depth interviews and analysed using thematic analysis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Two main themes emerged: meaning ascribed to suicide attempts and factors influencing suicide attempts. It was observed that adolescents viewed suicide as a means to alleviate their pains (physical, social, emotional and economic). Adolescents’ suicide attempts were primarily sparked by social variables including broken relationships, fear of stigma associated with sickness, rejection by the community and familial concerns such as financial hardships, rejection, trust issues and unfulfilled family expectations.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study shows the social and familial factors influencing adolescent suicide attempts in Ghana. The findings emphasize the need for comprehensive mental health support, community-based interventions and strategies to address these specific risk factors.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12888-024-06279-w.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2024

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Suicide among adolescents is a major public health crisis worldwide. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), suicide is the second leading cause of death globally among individuals aged 15–29 years, with nearly 800,000 suicide deaths annually [1]. Though suicide rates have traditionally been highest in high-income countries, almost 79% of global suicides now occur in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) partly due to a lack of mental health resources [1]. In the African region, suicide rates among adolescents and youth have been rising steadily over the past few decades [2].

In Ghana, suicide represents a significant but overlooked public health burden, especially among adolescents and youth. The country currently lacks comprehensive data on the national prevalence and risk factors for suicidal behaviours. However, limited research suggests that suicide is one of the leading causes of mortality among adolescents aged 15–19 years in Ghana [3]. Studies indicate higher suicide rates among urban versus rural youth and a preponderance among males [4]. The means of suicide are predominantly hanging and poisoning, although drowning and firearm-related suicides are also reported [3].

The aetiology of suicidal behaviour is complex, with individual, family, community and sociocultural factors interacting to confer vulnerability [5]. Family factors such as parenting practices, parent-child relationships and exposure to adversity including violence, neglect and parental psychopathology have been strongly implicated in suicidal ideation and attempts among adolescents worldwide [6, 7]. Evidence shows that experiencing child maltreatment, family dysfunction, parental mental illness including depression, substance abuse and insecure attachment styles are significantly associated with an increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviours in adolescents across cultures [8]. A systematic review was conducted on self-harm with suicidal and non-suicidal intent in young people in sub-Saharan [9]. The study found that adolescents in the region exhibited varying rates of self-harm behaviours, with several risk factors including academic failure, sexual, emotional and physical abuse, problems in romantic relationships, family conflict, depression and past self-harm. Nonetheless, since non-suicide intentions of self-harm were included, it is difficult to delineate the risk factors for suicidal behaviours [9].

In Ghana, studies have linked family factors like parental divorce, polygamous family settings and intra-familial physical violence to increased suicidality among youth [3, 10]. However, there remains an acute scarcity of research showing the role of family-level influences in adolescent suicide in the Ghanaian context. This qualitative study explored familial and social factors associated with suicide attempts among adolescents aged 15–19 years in Techiman Municipality in the Bono East Region and Ningo Prampram in the Greater Accra Region of Ghana. The findings highlight the complex dynamics within families that may engender vulnerability to suicidal behaviours among Ghanaian youth. Understanding the contextual risk factors is key to informing culturally appropriate prevention strategies and interventions to address adolescent suicide in Ghana.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: This was a qualitative study using in-depth interviews to explore family and social factors associated with suicide attempts among adolescents aged 15–19.

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: The study was conducted at two sites: Techiman Municipality in the Bono East Region and Ningo-Prampram District in the Greater Accra Region of Ghana. Techiman Municipality is in the transitional forest-savannah zone with a population of 243,335. Ningo-Prampram District is in the coastal savannah zone with a population of 70,923. These locations were purposively selected to capture perspectives from diverse socio-cultural settings in Ghana. Techiman Municipality represents a blend of urban and rural characteristics with strong traditional family structures and predominantly agricultural livelihoods, while Ningo-Prampram District represents a coastal and peri-urban area with contrasting socio-cultural dynamics. This contrast allowed for the exploration of how different sociocultural contexts and urbanization levels might influence adolescent suicide attempts.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Participants were 20 adolescents aged 15–19 years with a recent history of attempted suicide recruited from health facilities and communities. Purposive sampling was used to ensure maximum variation across age, gender, education and other demographics.

sec.title: Recruitment
sec.p: In health facilities, mental health professionals identified eligible participants based on information from medical records and referrals. The health professionals reviewed records to identify eligible adolescents with a recent history of suicide attempts. When potential participants were identified, the health professionals initiated a contact to introduce the study while ensuring this process was sensitive to the ongoing care the eligible participants were receiving. Adolescents who expressed interest were then contacted by the research team to provide detailed information about the study and to obtain informed consent. The community recruitment strategy was implemented to recruit adolescents who might not have sought formal healthcare services. Community health nurses and youth organizations working with adolescents were engaged as gatekeepers due to their established trust and networks within the communities. These gatekeepers identified potential adolescents via their community networks through ongoing community mental health promotion initiatives. Information about the study was disseminated during these educational sessions, where adolescents learned about the objectives and significance of the research. Interested participants were given the option to directly contact the research team or have the community health workers facilitate the connection. This was done to ensure a supportive and accessible recruitment process.

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: In-depth interviews were conducted with participants using a semi-structured guide exploring factors leading to their suicide attempts. The interview guide used in this study is available as Supplementary File 1 [11]. Key areas covered were family relationships, home environment, school and peer experiences, mental health, precipitating events and perceptions of their attempt. Interviews were conducted in participants’ preferred language (Twi, Ga or English) by trained research assistants with experience in adolescent mental health research. Interviews lasted approximately 60–90 min and were conducted in private places (either at health facilities or community centres) based on participant preference Interviews conducted in local languages were translated into English by certified translators. Additionally, detailed field notes documenting non-verbal cues, environmental context and initial analytical insights were taken during and after each interview. All interviews were audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: Transcripts were analysed using inductive thematic analysis. Data were immersed through repeated reading of transcripts. Initial open coding was conducted to identify concepts which were categorized to generate themes. Themes were refined and connectivity between themes was explored using constant comparison. MAXQDA 2022 software facilitated data analysis. To ensure the trustworthiness of the data, we employed several strategies. Regular peer debriefing sessions with uninvolved colleagues provided external validation of the research process and interpretations. Also, an audit trail was documented for all research activities and decisions to allow for independent verification. We also employed member checking which involved participants reviewing and confirming the findings, enhancing the accuracy of the interpretations. Finally, we maintained reflexive journals to acknowledge and mitigate potential biases to ensure integrity and objectivity throughout the entire study.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The results section of our study presents the socio-demographic profiles of the participants and this is followed by the main findings which have been presented thematically. The results section of our study presents the socio-demographic profiles of the participants, and this followed by the main findings which have been presented thematically. In all, two major themes emerged from the study. These themes were: the meaning ascribed to suicide attempts and factors influencing suicide attempts.

sec.title: Demographic characteristics of respondents
sec.p: Table 1 shows that more than half 13 (65%) of the participants were aged 18 to 19 years whilst 7 (35%) were between the ages of 16 and 17 years. A majority (75%) of the participants were females. For the educational level of the participants, most (60%) had completed Senior High School (SHS) with the remaining 40% reported having attained some primary education. It was also observed that none of the participants were married. Regarding the religious affiliations of the participants, the majority (85%) were Christians with the remaining 25% being Muslims.

sec.title: Meaning ascribed to attempted suicide
sec.p:
Our findings revealed that attempted suicide was perceived as a failed attempt to end one’s own life from profound emotional distress, despair extreme, and hopelessness. It was perceived as the only means of escaping from unbearable circumstances. Therefore, when undesirable experiences left victims devastated and depressed, suicide was thought to be the last resort to ending trauma when all attempts to resolve the problems proved futile. Below are some responses from the study participants.
“Attempted suicide is for instance if you are with someone and maybe the person disappointed you or you have heard bad news which you do not want to hear leaving you devastated and you do not know what to do, you may try ending your life to bring the undesirable experiences to an end. When that attempt fails, then that is a suicide attempt.” (Male, Municipality B).
“If they say someone attempted suicide, it means the person has a problem and then he or she cannot deal with it then he tried to end his life but fortunately for him or her, someone came to save him or her. So that’s a suicidal attempt.” (Female, Municipality A).
“Attempted suicide is when someone plans to take his or her life or when someone wants to end his or her life because of something that has happened which has hurt the person so much. That is what I know about it.” (Male, Municipality A).

sec.title: Factors influencing suicide attempts
sec.p: Our findings revealed seven major risk factors that influenced the respondents’ suicidal attempts. While four of the factors emanated from the immediate environment, thus from the family, the other factors stemmed from broader social sources. Therefore, these factors have been classified into two sub-themes namely, familial and social factors.

sec.title: Familial factors
sec.p: Some familial factors predisposed the adolescents to attempt suicide. These factors included financial difficulties, rejection, trust issues and unmet family expectations. These familial factors are presented below with their illustrative quotes from the adolescents.

sec.title: Financial difficulties within the family
sec.p:
Financial difficulties experienced in some families affected their ability to assist their wards in achieving their goals. Struggles to access formal education included the inability to pay school fees and purchase necessary supplies due to the low financial status of their families. These challenges forced them to drop out of school or forgo higher educational opportunities. This led to the thought of limited prospects which exacerbated feelings of frustration and consequently suicide attempts. Below is an excerpt from a study participant.
“… After completing JHS, it was my dream to continue with my secondary level but then there was no financial support as my mother who I lived with did not have the means. I waited and waited then I started thinking, giving a second thought to certain things I may say. I waited a year in the house. Then I started to think that it was my destiny not to have a good future due to my inability to continue my education. Then the negative thought of suicide started popping in my mind.” (Female, Municipality B).
I felt pressured after senior high school when all my mates proceeded to tertiary. They continued their education and I was still in the house without hope of any help coming rom anywhere. I decided to end it all. I thought all hopes were lost and I had nothing.

sec.title: Rejection by the family
sec.p:
It was interesting to know that two respondents were rejected by their families due to superstitious beliefs. They were believed to have evil spirits which they were accused of using to destroy their families. They were therefore stigmatized, ostracized and mistreated on unfounded superstitions or misconceptions. The ill-treatment and rejection especially from their families which they thought should rather protect them from social harm had a devastating effect on their mental health and well-being. The combination of social rejection, emotional distress and perceived hopelessness for acceptance or reconciliation contributed to suicidal ideation and consequently an attempt. The following is an illustrative quote from one participant.
“…, a prophet said I have an evil spirit and a pot in my stomach that is why every day I complain of stomach ache, and I always fall sick to have my parents spend money… The people told my dad and my stepmom something false about me… Because of that they never liked me. My stepmother does things to push me away. So, every time I am alone because nobody wants to be close to me…I counted on my family because if everyone has rejected me, at least the family should have done something to show that they do not believe what was said. They were however the ones who were doing the worst. They worsen the situation… I felt rejected. I always wanted to be at a quiet place so whilst being at that place a thought came to me that since they do not want me in their midst again, then if I end my life, that will be it. It will make everybody happy and I felt that was the best way to solve that problem that I had.” (Female, Municipality A).

sec.title: Trust issues within the family
sec.p:
We also found that mistrust from family members also led a respondent to attempt suicide. The experience of being disbelieved or mistrusted by one’s family members, especially in instances of serious allegations such as sexual abuse, was profoundly traumatic and damaging to the mental health of the adolescents. This betrayal exacerbated their emotional distress and ultimately contributed suicidal attempts. In one instance, a victim of near sexual abuse was accused of wrongdoing by the perpetrator and the family of the victim believed the perpetrator’s side of the story. Below is an excerpt from a respondent.
“… We slept on the same bed…. whilst we were sleeping, something was touching me. I was not sure what it was so I checked and realised it was my uncle who was touching me with his hand… Then, it happened again the third time. That time he pulled my dress and I shouted… in fact I was shivering and my uncle said I should not tell anybody that he tried raising my dress or he used his hand to touch me… I later packed my things and I went to my mother. Then I told my mother everything that had happened in the house but she did not believe me… She said my uncle told her whilst I was with him that I have been cooking for a guy who lived in the same house then he told me not to cook for the man again and I got annoyed and left his house. My mother said she believed what my uncle told her. …In fact, that day I was very sad. So, I decided that, okay, I will end it.” (Female, Municipality A).

sec.title: Unmet family expectations
sec.p:
We discovered that some families had expectations specifically high educational outcomes for their wards. These expectations were driven by aspirations for success, social status, or a desire for their children to fulfil certain career paths. However, these expectations became unrealistic or unattainable due to the poor academic performance of the adolescents. The pressure mounted on these study participants by their families to meet the high academic expectations significantly affected their mental health. They felt their best efforts, were not acknowledged, or validated by their parents. This led to feelings of inadequacy and hopelessness and, consequently suicidal attempts. Below is an excerpt from one respondent.
“… The issue started from JHS. It was about my performance in school. It was bad. It started from JHS but in became worse in SHS and my parents got to know and their attention was on it… They tried to make feel I was not doing enough but I was doing enough. I knew I was doing enough…So the pressure was just too much…”

sec.title: Social factors
sec.p: Three main issues were found as social risk factors which influenced adolescents to attempt suicide. These include broken relationships, fear of morbidity-related stigmatization and rejection by the community.

sec.title: Broken relationships
sec.p:
Our findings revealed that failed romantic relationships were a major social factor which influenced the decision of more than half of the study participants to attempt suicide. They invested deeply in romantic relationships, emotionally, financially, and in terms of time and effort. When these investments were not reciprocated or appreciated by their partners but instead met with sudden breakups, they experienced intense emotional pain and struggled to make sense of the rejection. In other instances, some of the study participants became pregnant and their partners refused to take responsibility resulting in feelings of abandonment. These situations were overwhelming for the participants coupled with their inability to cope resulted in a suicide attempt. Below are excerpts from some of the study participants.
“… I go fishing to make money. I spend this money on the lady because I do not want to lose her. What I was pained about most was that she requested an iPhone which I bought for her. It pained me because I could not even go back for the phone after she jilted me. The thought of seeing the girl that I love with my classmate or colleague hurts… (Male, Municipality A).

sec.title: Rejection by the community
sec.p: We found that generally, the spread of negative perceptions and rumours about two of the study participants had a profound effect on them. The negative perceptions translated into stigmatisation, isolation and ostracization within the community. Being marginalized by their community members enforced the sense of being unwanted and unworthy of acceptance. For school-going study participants, there was a spillover into the school environment, affecting their academic performance, social interactions and sense of belonging. Peers in school did not want to associate with them further deepening their feelings of isolation. The cumulative impact of rejection experienced across multiple domains of life was overwhelming and contributed to suicide attempts as they perceived suicide as the only way to escape from rejection.

Some of the excerpts from the respondents are stated below.
“… I don’t know how come the whole community too has heard that I have an evil spirit and then they started going away from me, pushing me away from them. So that made me very sad as nobody liked me… Nobody, not even the kids within the environment even school wanted to play with me. I was alone every day.” (Female, Municipality A).
“… I was perceived to bring people bad luck. Due to that, I was maltreated at home. The kind of treatment I was going through at home made me an unhappy man on earth. I received maltreatment at school too so I had no friends at school. I walked alone and did everything alone. So, I felt like there is no hope for me on earth or no essence for me to stay on earth. (Male, Municipality A).

sec.title: Fear of morbidity-related stigmatization
sec.p:
We also found that fear of stigmatization led to the decision of a study participant to attempt suicide. When her partner was diagnosed with HIV, she experienced heightened anxiety and distress. Even before knowing her own HIV status, she was already anticipating potential discrimination and stigma related to HIV/AIDS. The fear of being stigmatized by society due to HIV/AIDS resulted in suicidal thoughts to escape the anticipated humiliation and discrimination. When asked what led her to contemplate suicide, she explained:
“…my guy was diagnosed with HIV… I felt I had also contracted it. I saw the way they treat people within the society who are HIV positive. They don’t include them in social gatherings. They stigmatize them. They humiliate them. So, I was thinking, what if I just end my own life, maybe that will solve it all. Because if they do not know about it, there is no way they will speak or stigmatize against me. (Female, Municipality B)

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our study shows the complex web of individual, familial and social factors that influence suicide attempts among Ghanaian adolescents. The findings show two major themes which require detailed examination: the sociocultural context of suicidal behaviour in Ghanaian adolescents and the intersection of familial and dynamics and social influences in suicide risk. These provide current information into both the unique aspects of adolescent suicide attempts in Ghana and their connection to broader global patterns.

sec.title: Sociocultural context and meaning-making in adolescent suicide attempts
sec.p: The meanings ascribed to suicide attempts by Ghanaian adolescents show a complex interaction between psychological distress and sociocultural factors. Participants viewed suicide attempts as a means to end emotional suffering and a last resort when problems become overwhelming. This agrees with previous literature which found adolescents often see suicide as an escape from unbearable psychological pain and a way to end their distress [12, 13]. The desire to escape adverse life’s circumstances is a key driver of suicidal behaviour among youth worldwide [14].

In the cultural context of Ghana, attempted suicide may represent a maladaptive coping strategy adopted by vulnerable adolescents struggling to manage social adversity. Qualitative studies conducted in Ghana have revealed a perception of suicide being an option for those who have exhausted other solutions for their problems [15, 16]. This suggests a cultural framework where suicide represents not just individual distress, but a breakdown of traditional support systems and coping mechanisms. Taken together, these findings indicate the need for preventive interventions to promote more adaptive coping skills among Ghanaian adolescents. Teaching problem-solving, emotion regulation, distress tolerance and help-seeking could mitigate the risk of viewing suicide as the only way out during a crisis or experience of traumatic events.

Also noteworthy is how supernatural beliefs and stigma intersect with suicidal behaviour in this context. Our findings revealed cases where adolescents were labelled as having “evil spirits”, leading to family rejection and community ostracization. This demonstrates how traditional beliefs can amplify psychological distress and social isolation, creating a complex web of risk factors unique to the Ghanaian context. This might have influenced the perspectives of adolescents who experienced intense psychological pain and hopelessness preceding suicide attempts. This is consistent with the interpersonal theory of suicide, which posits that suicidal desire emerges from feeling socially alienated and perceiving oneself as a burden to others [17, 18]. Alienation is a known correlate of suicidality among the youth [19]. Improving social connectedness and fostering belongingness could therefore buffer against the development of suicidal ideation among vulnerable Ghanaian adolescents. Furthermore, the findings reveal suicide attempts often stem from disappointing interpersonal experiences, showing the central role of relationships. Past evidence has also linked conflictual family dynamics and peer problems to suicidality in Ghanaian adolescents [20]. Interventions should therefore target strengthening family and peer relationships as protective factors.

sec.title: The intersection of family dynamics and social influence
sec.p: Our findings reveal a complex interplay between family dynamics and social influences that creates particularly vulnerable conditions for adolescents. This interaction manifests in several key dimensions:

sec.title: Economic vulnerability and education aspirations
sec.p: In exploring the factors influencing suicide attempts, our findings reveal financial hardship as a predominant family-level stressor contributing to suicide attempts among adolescents. Financial difficulties experienced within some families significantly impacted the respondents’ ability to pursue their educational goals. Many respondents faced the harsh reality of not being able to continue their education due to their parent’s inability to pay their school fees. The stress and pressure from not being able to advance academically, coupled with the lack of financial support, led to negative thoughts and ultimately suicide attempts. A recent study has shown that financial stress is a leading catalyst for suicide and people who experience stress from financial issues are 20 times more likely to attempt suicide than those who do not [32].

Financial strain is a significant risk factor for suicidal ideation and suicide attempts, and economic and financial uncertainty is associated with higher suicide rates [33, 34]. Financial strain within the family can lead to feelings of hopelessness and despair, especially when educational aspirations are hindered. For the adolescents in our study, the inability to continue education due to financial constraints represented more than just an economic challenge - it symbolized shattered dreams and limited prospects within Ghana’s education-centred social mobility framework. Thus, the inability to access education can be profoundly distressing, increasing the risk of suicidal ideation and attempts [35]. This finding therefore suggests suicide prevention strategies must consider broader economic interventions and educational support systems.

sec.title: Family dynamics and emotional invalidation
sec.p: The current study revealed that some adolescents experienced rejection by their families, often due to superstitious beliefs or other reasons. Such rejection led to ill-treatment and emotional distress, ultimately contributing to the decision to attempt suicide. This has also been reported earlier showing that parental rejection is a significant risk factor for suicidal behaviours among young individuals [36]. Adolescents who feel unloved or unwanted by their families may develop feelings of hopelessness, which increases the likelihood of suicidal ideation and attempts [37]. A study on social rejection and suicidal ideation and attempts among adolescents following hospitalization found that social rejection-related life events are associated with a heightened risk for prospective suicide attempts [38]. Social rejection within family systems creates a cascading effect on adolescent mental health. Also, another study exploring the family factors associated with suicide attempts among adolescents and young adults found that perceived criticisms from parents, invalidation from family members and low levels of perceived family support may contribute to suicidal behaviour [36].

The emotional invalidation experienced by these adolescents extends beyond simple rejection, manifesting as a systematic breakdown of family support systems. Our findings further suggest that the cultural context adds additional layers of complexity. The influence of superstitious beliefs in family rejection represents a distinct cultural dimension that may require specialized intervention approaches. The current study emphasizes the need for families to provide a supportive and nurturing environment for their children, fostering a sense of belonging and emotional security. This is because the intersection of cultural beliefs and family rejection presents a unique perspective on how traditional beliefs can compound psychological distress in adolescents.

sec.title: Broken bonds: trust and communication patterns
sec.p: The erosion of trust within family systems emerged as another critical pathway to suicidal behaviour, revealing complex patterns of communication breakdown and invalidation. The findings reveal a lack of trust and belief in false accusations by parents as key family dynamics contributing to suicide attempts among the respondents. Being blamed for behaviours they denied, without being listened to or believed, fostered a sense of betrayal that eroded the parent-child bond. Some previous findings have also linked poor parent-child communication and fractured relationships to heightened suicidal risk among youth [39]. It also agrees with evidence that family distrust predicts suicide attempts among adolescents [40]. This betrayal of the fundamental parent-child bond appears to have far-reaching implications for adolescent mental health.

Previous research has underscored the significance of family support and open communication in preventing suicide among adolescents [41]. Adolescents who feel heard and validated by their families are more likely to seek help and cope with distressing situations in healthier ways. Thus, fostering trust and open dialogue within the family is crucial for promoting mental resilience and reducing suicide risk. Furthermore, parents siding with alleged perpetrators over their own sexually abused children produced profound distress and suicidal behaviours. This mirrors prior research indicating that parental disbelief of abuse disclosures engenders trauma and self-harming in youth [42]. Together, these findings reveal the detrimental effects of broken trust and disbelief by parents on adolescent mental health. Interventions targeting improved parental communication, emotional validation and family counselling are critically needed to strengthen parent-child relationships and mitigate suicide risk stemming from family distrust.

sec.title: Academic pressure and family expectations
sec.p: Unmet educational expectations were another important family-based stressor contributing to suicidal behaviours among the adolescents. High parental pressures to succeed academically, coupled with perceived failure to meet those expectations, fostered a sense of shame and worthlessness culminating in suicide attempts. This is consistent with earlier research showing that Ghanaian youth may experience suicidal thoughts if they are unable to meet the high academic standards set by their parents [16]. Additionally, it also agrees with earlier research that showed a cross-cultural association between increased suicide risk and perceived academic failure [43]. Our findings suggest that the pressure stems not just from academic performance itself, but from the broader cultural implications of academic success or failure. The shame and worthlessness experienced by adolescents appear to be magnified by cultural expectations around family reputation and social standing. Furthermore, the distress appeared to stem from a disconnect between parent’s and children’s appraisal of academic efforts.

Moreover, the study shows a critical communication gap between parents and adolescents regarding academic effort and achievement. While adolescents reported giving their best effort, parents’ continuous pressure for improvement suggests a fundamental misalignment in how academic success is defined and measured. This misalignment, coupled with cultural emphasis on academic achievement, creates a particularly challenging environment for adolescent mental health. The ensuing self-blame and hopelessness tipped the balance towards life-ending acts being viewed as the only way out. These results highlight how important it is for parents to support their children’s best academic performance by setting reasonable expectations, appreciating small victories and employing constructive motivational techniques. Parent and child views may align through interventions that support candid communication and cooperative goal-setting in education.

sec.title: Romantic relationships and suicidal behaviour
sec.p: We also found failed romantic relationships as a predominant social stressor precipitating suicidal behaviours among the adolescents in our study. Being jilted or dumped engendered profound feelings of rejection, worthlessness and betrayal, often culminating in suicide being viewed as the only escape from emotional anguish. This is consistent with previous works linking relationship problems and breakups to heightened risk for suicidal ideation and attempts among adolescents [8, 44].

The study revealed particularly gendered dimensions of relationship-related distress. For female adolescents, the combination of pregnancy and relationship abandonment created compound vulnerabilities, showing how reproductive responsibility becomes disproportionately feminized. Unequal power dynamics leaving girls solely accountable for out-of-wedlock pregnancies has been linked to suicidal behaviours across cultures [45]. Pregnancy denial following intimacy also constituted a profound relational betrayal, mirroring previous findings of breaches of trust predicting suicidality after breakups [46].

For adolescent boys, partner infidelity with trusted friends engendered severe trauma and suicidal acts. While existing literature shows the link between partner infidelity and suicide risk [47], our findings suggest that the public nature of betrayal within close social networks intensifies this effect. The intersection of masculinity, honour and public humiliation emerges as a crucial consideration in understanding male adolescent suicidal behaviour. These findings emphasise the need for preventive efforts addressing healthy relationships and coping skills to manage relationship stress adaptively. Strengthening youth resilience while tackling harmful gender dynamics is important to reducing suicidal risk stemming from relationship breakdowns.

sec.title: Health-related stigma and suicidal behaviour
sec.p: This study also revealed anticipated HIV stigma as a sociocultural factor promoting suicidal behaviours among adolescents. Despite being HIV-negative, the fear of being shunned and ostracized due to a partner’s seropositive status engendered profound hopelessness and suicide attempts. This observation extends beyond the known impacts of actual HIV stigma and shows how the mere prospect of stigmatization can become an important suicide risk factor.

This fear of social isolation and humiliation pushed the participant to contemplate suicide to avoid the perceived consequences of an HIV diagnosis. Stigma surrounding HIV and mental health issues can have severe implications for affected individuals, leading to feelings of shame, hopelessness and suicidal thoughts [48]. Perceived stigma has been associated with social isolation, depression and emotional distress among HIV-affected adolescents in Ghana [49]. The anticipatory nature of stigma-related distress suggests deep-rooted social attitudes that require intervention at both individual and community levels. The findings reveal that fear of social devaluation can become so intense that death appears preferable to living with stigma, emphasizing the urgent need for community-level stigma reduction initiatives alongside individual support services.

sec.title: Community integration and social rejection
sec.p: Our findings reveal how rejection operates across multiple social systems, creating compounding effects on adolescent suicide risk. Notably, rejection within the community was often an extension of the rejection experienced within their families. Family dynamics have a profound influence on adolescents’ mental health [41]. When individuals face rejection and ill-treatment within their families, it can lead to feelings of worthlessness, isolation and loneliness. These negative emotions can then spill over into other areas of their lives, including interactions within the community and at school, ultimately perpetuating a cycle of rejection and alienation. A hostile family environment, faulty interactions between family members and a lack of perceived family support may contribute to suicidal behaviour [36]. The impact of rejection on adolescents’ mental health is significant, as it affects their self-esteem, academic performance and social relationships [35]. The current study’s findings also indicate that the rejection experienced by respondents in their communities further compounded their sense of loneliness and despair. Rejected and ostracized, they struggled to find support and friendship, which reinforced their belief that they were unwanted and unloved. Interventions aimed at promoting mental health and well-being among adolescents should consider the broader social context and the role of relationships and rejection in shaping their mental health outcomes. Building supportive environments, fostering healthy relationships and providing accessible mental health support can be essential in mitigating the risk of suicide attempts among vulnerable adolescents.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our findings show the interplay of family and social factors in adolescent suicidal behaviours through the lens of Bronfenbrenner’s Ecological Systems Theory [50, 51]. Within this theory, we observed how suicide risk emerges from interactions across multiple systems: from microsystem factors (family dynamics, romantic relationships) to macrosystem influences (cultural beliefs, societal attitudes). The findings are also consistent with the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide [18], particularly how thwarted belongingness manifests through family rejection and social alienation in the Ghanaian context. The study reveals definite pathways through which cultural and contextual factors shape suicidal behaviour among Ghanaian adolescents. Financial difficulties, spiritual abuse and educational pressures operated within unique cultural matrices, creating context-specific vulnerabilities. These findings extend Family Systems Theory [52, 53] by showing how traditional Ghanaian family structures and beliefs mediate the relationship between family dysfunction and suicidal behaviour.

Our analysis suggests that suicidal behaviour emerges when culturally influenced protective factors are eroded while risk factors accumulate across ecological levels. This understanding calls for culturally informed interventions that will strengthen family systems while respecting traditional structures, address economic vulnerabilities through sustainable community-based approaches, transform harmful cultural practices while preserving protective cultural elements and build capacity for mental health support within existing community frameworks.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This study’s key strength lies in its qualitative methodology, which enabled an in-depth exploration of the complex lived experiences of adolescents who attempted suicide in Ghana, providing a rich contextual understanding through rigorous thematic analysis and team-based interpretation. While our findings provide current information for suicide prevention, it is not without limitations. The retrospective nature of interviews may have influenced participants’ recall and interpretation of events. Also, despite careful attention to trustworthiness through member checking and peer debriefing, the cultural stigma surrounding suicide may have influenced the depth of experiences shared by some participants. Future research might benefit from longitudinal designs to capture how risk factors evolve and participatory approaches that engage communities in developing culturally appropriate suicide prevention strategies.

sec.title: Electronic supplementary material
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: The original version of this article was revised: the title, text and abbreviationshave been corrected.
Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
A Correction to this paper has been published: 10.1186/s12888-025-06870-9

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors are grateful to the participants for sharing their experiences.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Conceptualization and design: SG, JKB & RA; Analysis, interpretation of data: LK & WKA; drafting of manuscript: WKA, SG and NK. All authors approved the final version of the paper.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets created and analysed during the current study are not publicly available because doing so could violate the privacy and confidentiality of study participants but are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: Ethical clearance was obtained from the University of Health and Allied Sciences Research Ethics Committee (UHAS-REC B.10[043]22–23). This study adheres to the guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki. Permission was sought from local authorities before conducting the study. Informed consent was taken from all participants affirming voluntariness, privacy and anonymity.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
